data.title: TE Connectivity expects Q3 sales of $5B and adjusted EPS of around $2.83 as AI revenue outlook rises by $150M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TE Connectivity (TEL) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • "Our second quarter sales were over $4.7 billion" and "we delivered record adjusted earnings per share of $2.73" (CEO Terrence Curtin).

  • "In the second quarter, we had record orders of over $5 billion" and "we announced that our Board approved a 10% increase to our quarterly cash dividend" (CEO Curtin).

  • "Orders were $5.3 billion with a book-to-bill of 1.12" and "over 70% of the company's order growth was in the Industrial segment" (CEO Curtin).

  • "In our digital data networks, we had another outstanding quarter" and "we acquired a leading technology for passive optical connectivity solutions" (CEO Curtin).

  • "We now expect our AI revenues in fiscal 2026 to be about $150 million higher than our view 90 days ago" and "this entire increase will be in the second half of the year" (CEO Curtin).

  • "For the quarter, we achieved adjusted operating income of over $1 billion and adjusted operating margins of 21.7%" (CFO Heath Mitts).

Outlook

  • "As we look to the third quarter, we are expecting third quarter sales to be $5 billion" and "we expect adjusted earnings per share to be up 17% year-over-year to around $2.83" (CEO Terrence Curtin).

  • "We expect Q3 [the adjusted effective tax rate] to be around 23% and the full year tax rate to be approximately 22%" (CFO Heath Mitts).

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s outlook language, management shifted from guiding Q2 sales of $4.7 billion and adjusted EPS of around $2.65 to guiding Q3 sales of $5 billion and adjusted EPS of around $2.83 (CEO Curtin).

Financial results

  • "Our second quarter sales were over $4.7 billion" and "sales grew 15% on a reported basis and 7% organically year-over-year" (CEO Terrence Curtin).

  • "We delivered record adjusted earnings per share of $2.73" and "our operating margins on an adjusted basis were 22%" (CEO Curtin).

  • "GAAP operating income was $954 million" and it "included $8 million of acquisition-related charges, $10 million of restructuring and other charges and $57 million of amortization expense" (CFO Heath Mitts).

  • "GAAP EPS was $2.90 for the quarter" and it "included a $0.39 tax benefit primarily related to a settlement of prior period tax matters" (CFO Mitts).

  • "Cash from operations was $947 million and free cash flow was $680 million" and "through the first half of the fiscal year, free cash flow was a record $1.3 billion" (CFO Mitts).

Q&A

  • Scott Davis, Melius Research LLC: Can you talk about the $150 million bump up and when it ships? CEO Curtin: "the $150 million that I mentioned... relate to the second half" and "it will ramp in the second half."

  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: Can record orders be sustained and what did TE see in April amid geopolitical volatility? CEO Curtin: "in the first month since quarter end, the order momentum continues to be very strong" and "we have not seen any demand negative impacts."

  • Luke Junk, Baird: Updated view on copper vs. optical in AI and the RAM Photonics deal? CEO Curtin: "it's not copper or optical, it's copper and optical" and the acquisition "enables advancements in high-density fiber array connections."

  • Amit Daryanani, Evercore ISI: What’s driving energy growth and commercial transportation strength? CEO Curtin: "probably about 60% or 2/3 of what we do is around utility and grid hardening" and commercial transportation is driven by "continued share gains from new program wins and increasing content per vehicle."

  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA: What drives expected acceleration in auto content growth, and what’s the sequential DDN order trend? CEO Curtin: "we expect there to be 88 million to 89 million units" and on sequential DDN orders, "we can follow up later to your question. I just don't have it here in front of me."

  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer: Portfolio actions, sensors, and buybacks vs. M&A? CEO Curtin: "we really like the portfolio" and it is "more about playing offense." CFO Mitts: "the pipeline is actually for M&A, it's actually -- it's pretty active right now" and "stay tuned."

  • Joseph Spak, UBS: How do inflation pressures affect margins next quarter? CFO Mitts: "we do have a playbook" and "there is a little bit of noise in our absolute margins for sure on that."

  • Joseph Giordano, TD Cowen: Is there a lag between pricing in orders and the P&L, and any update on CapEx? CFO Mitts: "we have taken our CapEx up this year" and "you should expect CapEx this year to be to run about 6% of revenue."

  • Guy Hardwick, Barclays: Are enterprise/telecom IT budgets being squeezed by AI spending? CEO Curtin: "we are seeing nice growth" in enterprise, telecom and wireless, though "it's not at the rates like I talked about 60%."

  • Asiya Merchant, Citigroup: Any supply tightness in CPUs/GPUs/memory affecting visibility and ramps? CEO Curtin: "we don't buy that" and "we're not seeing any slowdowns... If anything... they're increasing their ramps."

  • Steven Fox, Fox Advisors: How durable is energy growth over coming quarters? CEO Curtin: "with what's happening in utility, grid hardening and the industrial/data center space, it's full steam ahead" while "the biggest lumpiness" is "in the clean energy space."

  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: Is optical additive to the AI content opportunity over time? CEO Curtin: "this will help us in the scale-out element" and "it's an increase of content that we have."

  • Colin Langan, Wells Fargo: Downside if customers switch from copper to fiber? CEO Curtin: "it's not going to be an or, it's going to be an and" and "our TAM and copper is going to continue to grow due to the power elements that you need."

  • William Stein, Truist: Why has DDN revenue been flat sequentially despite bookings, and why did Industrial margins dip sequentially? CEO Curtin: "we're going to be stepping up here in the second half" and "the $3 billion... continues to shift towards the left." CFO Mitts: "you're going to have -- always have a little bit of noise in any quarter" and referenced "ramped some investments in our DDN business."

  • Shreyas Patil, Wolfe Research: How big is optical for AI today and where else bolster via M&A? CEO Curtin: "we're going to stay in passives" and "we will continue to look at via partnerships versus our organic development."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive overall, with persistent probing on AI durability, copper-vs.-optical transition risk, and margin sensitivity; one exchange pressed for downside framing (Colin Langan, Wells Fargo: "any way to frame the downside or the content loss").

  • Management: positive in prepared remarks and generally confident in Q&A, repeatedly pointing to backlog and orders; CEO Curtin said, "we expect our strong performance will continue," and emphasized, "it's not copper or optical, it's copper and optical."

  • Versus last quarter, the Q&A remained AI-centric, but shifted more toward optical roadmap specifics and capital deployment (M&A pipeline and CapEx), with a clearer instance of a deferred detail (CEO Curtin: "we can follow up later").

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Management raised its fiscal 2026 AI revenue outlook again, but the incremental uplift was smaller than the prior quarter’s update: Q2 call "about $150 million higher" vs. Q1 call "$200 million more" than the prior view 90 days earlier (CEO Terrence Curtin).

  • Industrial growth messaging stayed centered on AI and energy, while the Q2 call added a specific optical-related acquisition update: "we acquired a leading technology for passive optical connectivity solutions" (CEO Curtin).

  • Analysts stayed focused on reconciling bookings to revenue, but Q2 featured more direct scrutiny around optical transition downside and sequential margin movement; management leaned on long-term framing (CFO Mitts: "We tend to look at margins on a year-to-date or rolling basis").

Risks and concerns

  • "We continue to operate in a dynamic environment" and management cited "increased inflationary pressures across certain input costs such as oil-based resins and freight charges" tied to "higher energy costs and broader geopolitical tensions" (CFO Heath Mitts).

  • Mitigation actions were described as "optimization of our factory footprint, targeted pricing actions and ongoing productivity initiatives" and a "localization strategy around supply chain" (CFO Mitts).

  • For visibility and timing risk, management repeated that AI-related orders and ramps can be uneven: "there will be some lumpiness" (CEO Terrence Curtin).

Final takeaway

Management emphasized that Q2 combined record adjusted EPS of $2.73 with record orders of $5.3 billion, while raising its fiscal 2026 AI revenue outlook by $150 million, all of which it tied to second-half ramps and backlog extending into 2027. For Q3, management guided to $5 billion of sales and adjusted EPS of around $2.83, while also flagging a more dynamic cost backdrop (resins and freight) that it said it is managing through pricing, footprint actions, and productivity levers, alongside an AI-driven CapEx level of about 6% of revenue.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Inspired Entertainment lands a key iGaming approval in Alberta
data.description:
Inspired Entertainment (INSE) announced on Wednesday that it has been approved by the Alberta Gaming, Liquor and Cannabis Commission and obtained registration as an iGaming Goods or Services Supplier—Critical Gaming Systems. The company said the approval positions it to support operators in Alberta when the regulated iGaming market launches and help strengthen its  presence across North America.

Notably, Alberta will become only the second Canadian province to introduce a regulated commercial online casino and sports betting market, with a planned launch date in Q3. Inspired Entertainment (INSE) is already active in the province through its partnership with Play Alberta, the government-run iGaming platform. It was noted that the Play Alberta live experience gives the company valuable insight into the market, the regulatory environment, and player preferences.

Shares of Inspired Entertainment (INSE) are down 24.4% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Skyworks on track to end in green for eighth straight session
data.description:
Skyworks Solutions (SWKS) was on track to log its eighth straight session of gains on Wednesday as the stock advanced 3.14% to $61.82 during afternoon trading.

Between April 13 and April 21, SWKS gained over 6%. On a YTD basis, however, it has lost 3.19% compared to a 3% rise in the broader markets.

The stock advanced on Wednesday after it was upgraded at  Barclays to Overweight from Equalweight. The brokerage also raised its price target of the stock to $100 from $95.

As per Seeking Alpha’s quant rating, the stock has a Hold rating with a score of 3.24 out of 5. SWKS has been rated an A- for profitability, an A for valuation, but an F for growth. Wall Street analysts had the same rating for the stock. However, Seeking Alpha analysts issued a Buy call.

Seeking Alpha analyst Wolf Report issued a Hold rating for SWKS earlier this month, arguing that the firm faces structural headwinds from its heavy Apple dependence, declining margins, and lack of AI exposure.

In the past five years, Skyworks’ revenue and net income have dropped sharply, with gross margins down 800 bps and net income down over 60%, it noted.

“Due to its lack of exposure to AI, the company lacks any sort of AI catalyst. It's in the old-school analogue camp, which is not only not seeing a growth surge, but it's also unlikely to see any sort of growth surge (serious one) in the near term,” the analyst commented.

data.title: Freddie Mac to begin accepting mortgage loans assessed through VantageScore 4.0
data.description:
Freddie Mac (FMCC) said on Wednesday that it will begin accepting mortgage loans assessed using the credit scoring model VantageScore 4.0.

The move will initially be implemented through a limited rollout with approved lenders.

Additionally, similar implementation efforts are underway for FICO Score 10T, another approved credit score model.

Approved lenders can choose between VantageScore 4.0 or Classic FICO through a tri-merge credit report for originating new mortgage loans and for loans submitted to Freddie Mac's automated underwriting system, Loan Product Advisor.

Lenders not participating in the limited rollout must continue to use Classic FICO scores from each bureau through a tri-merge credit report.

"With today's announcement, we are accepting a credit score model that incorporates additional data, including on-time rent payment history," said Freddie Mac's executive vice president and head of single-family acquisitions, Sonu Mittal.

data.title: United Airlines' guidance spooks Wall Street despite outlook to recover fuel spikes -- analysts
data.description:
United Airlines’ (UAL) fuel-adjusted profit outlook for the year continued to weigh on shares Wednesday even as the company downplayed any long-term negative impact from higher jet fuel.

“Moments of uncertainty for the airline industry may also create opportunity for United,” said United Airlines CEO Scott Kirby. “We have demonstrated quarter after quarter that we are built to withstand disruptions, and this moment is no different.”

Despite higher operating costs, United’s (UAL) profit increased 31% year-over-year in the first quarter even with a 5.9% increase in cost per available seat mile excluding fuel ((CASM-ex)). The gains were largely driven by strong passenger revenue per available seat mile ((PRASM)) across all regions and continued strength in corporate travel.

Still, guidance tarnished what was otherwise a solid quarter. Even though United (UAL) expects to fully recapture higher fuel costs by the fourth quarter of this year through fare increases and strategic capacity reductions (focused on Chicago’s O’Hare), “management acknowledged that lower capacity could pressure CASM-ex in the back half of the year,” said Morgan Stanley’s Ravi Shanker.

United (UAL) lowered its FY25 adjusted EPS guidance to $7.00 to $11.00 from $12 to $14, assuming capacity growth that is flat to up 2% in the third and fourth quarters. This also assumes 70% to 80% fuel price recovery in Q3 and up to 100% in Q4.

“Our sense is that the guidance bakes in UAL’s regular conservatism,” noted BofA Securities’ Andrew Didora, and by cutting unprofitable routes, the carrier can preserve high yields.

Added UBS analyst Atul Maheswari, “The proactive capacity cuts for H2 were particularly encouraging to us as they highlight the company’s focus on preserving margins and cash in an uncertain backdrop.”

Higher fares are already cushioning the blow from spiking jet fuel, specifically international fares, where “substantial” price increases are covering “more of the burden from rising fuel costs than domestic flights.” United (UAL) has raised fares five times in the first quarter alone.

“The longer the fuel costs are elevated, the higher fares will go and the longer they will last,” executives said on the company’s earnings call with analysts.

data.title: International Business Machines declines, ending seven-day rally
data.description:
International Business Machines (NYSE: IBM) ended a seven-session winning streak on Wednesday, with its shares down 2.75% at $248.64 in afternoon trading.

Over the prior seven sessions, the stock had rallied more than 10.80%, outperforming the S&P 500 Index, which rose 3.62%. Despite the recent run-up, IBM shares remain down about 13.31% in 2026, lagging the broader index’s 3.19% gain over the same period.

In a recent report, Brent Thill said IBM appears to be “picking up speed” ahead of its first-quarter results, citing potential upside in software driven by deal timing and steady Red Hat growth, while noting expectations for moderate revenue growth and margin expansion; he lowered his price target to $320 from $370, maintaining a Buy rating.

Separately, Seeking Alpha analyst Stone Fox Capital said IBM faces as much risk from AI-driven disruption as it does opportunity from AI-led growth, challenging the earlier bullish narrative. The analyst noted that IBM’s AI-related bookings remain concentrated in lower-margin consulting, limiting meaningful revenue and margin expansion, while forecasting modest growth of about 5% in 2026 revenue and 7% in earnings. Despite a sharp decline this year, the valuation does not fully reflect constrained AI upside and potential pressure on legacy businesses, and he reported a Sell rating.

Similarly, The Alpha analyst retained a Hold rating on IBM, citing uncertainty from AI commoditization that could weigh on its growth and margin outlook. While the software and infrastructure segments have shown solid recent momentum, the analyst flagged potential headwinds from evolving AI capabilities and pricing pressures, adding that the absence of clear structural catalysts supports a wait-and-watch stance.
 
Seeking Alpha's Quant rating  has a neutral stance on IBM with a Hold rating and a score of 3.13 out of 5, reflecting an A+ for profitability, offset by an F for growth and a C for momentum.
 

According to Wall Street analysts, 12 rated the stock a Buy or above, 7 rated it a Hold, while 2 considered it a Sell or lower.

Seeking Alpha analysts  also maintain a cautious stance, with a Hold rating on the stock.

data.title: Lockheed Martin set to extend losing streak to seven straight sessions
data.description:
Lockheed Martin (LMT) is set to extend its losing streak to seven consecutive sessions, with the stock trading 3.3% lower at $553.14 in afternoon trading on Wednesday.

The company’s shares have declined nearly 6.5% over the past six sessions. The stock has also fallen nearly 11.5% over the past month.

However, the stock has gained over 16.7% so far this year, outperforming the benchmark S&P 500, which has risen 3.2% in the same period.

Earlier today, the defense giant invested $25M in Fortem Technologies as part of the company’s Series B fundraising round, deepening its existing partnership focused on countering unmanned aerial system threats.

Looking at Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating, LMT has a Buy rating with a score of 4.32 out of 5. The company has been graded A+ for profitability and A- for revisions but is weighed down by a C grade in valuation and growth.

Seeking Alpha analysts are bullish, assigning the stock a Buy rating.

Seeking Alpha analyst Burak Kaan Yilmaz argued that Lockheed Martin is positioned for ~35% upside, supported by a DCF-derived target price of $843.21, driven by a $193.6B backlog and robust global defense demand.

“LMT's growth is underpinned by rising military spending, especially in missile defense and AI-enabled systems, amid increasing global conflicts and technological shifts,” Yilmaz said, adding that the company’s financial strength is evident with $6.9B in free cash flow, a conservative capital structure with 87.5% equity, a low 5.15% cost of debt, and consistent dividends plus buybacks.

Turning to the Wall Street community, six of 22 analysts rate LMT a Buy or better, 15 recommend Hold, and one rates it a Strong Sell.

data.title: RFK Jr. says no hand in rejecting Replimune’s lead asset
data.description:
At a Senate hearing, U.S. Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. said on Wednesday that he "had nothing to do” with the FDA’s decision to reject Replimune’s (REPL) lead candidate RP1 earlier this month.

The Massachusetts-based biotech has lost more than half of its market capitalization since April 10, when the FDA, for the second time in less than a year, declined to approve RP1, designed to target a skin cancer known as melanoma.

"This decision comes out of the FDA, and we trust ​the process there,” Kennedy said at the hearing to discuss the Trump administration’s budget for the HHS for fiscal ​year 2027. RFK Jr. cited the FDA commissioner, Marty Makary, as the official responsible for the rejection.

“And I've been told by Marty Makary that every panel that looked at that drug unanimously voted against it... because it does not appear to work,” he added.

Dear readers, we recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: EQT targets $5B net debt by year-end while embedding 10-15 Bcf of Q2 curtailments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: EQT Corporation (EQT) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We generated more than $1.8 billion of free cash flow in the first quarter, another record-high for EQT." (President, CEO & Director Toby Rice)
  • "With leverage now below 1x net debt to EBITDA and our long-term $5 billion net debt target within reach by year-end, EQT has entered a new chapter." (President, CEO & Director Rice)
  • "Despite the challenging weather conditions presented by Winter Storm Fern... production for the quarter came in above the high end of our guidance range." (President, CEO & Director Rice)
  • "If our LNG portfolio was fully online today, with current TTF and JKM spreads to Henry Hub, our projected 2026 free cash flow would be approximately $6 billion." (President, CEO & Director Rice)
  • "We delivered sales volumes above the high end of guidance into peak winter pricing, while our cash operating expenses and capital costs came in below the low end of guidance due to improved efficiencies." (Chief Financial Officer Jeremy Knop)
  • "As promised, we allocated post-dividend free cash flow to strengthening our balance sheet and retired more than $1.7 billion of senior notes during the quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)

Outlook

  • EQT did not provide EPS or revenue guidance figures in this transcript; guidance discussion focused on volumes, curtailments, capital timing, and leverage trajectory.
  • "After surging production volumes into peak winter pricing in Q1, we began tactically curtailing volumes this month to optimize price realizations during shoulder season and have embedded 10 to 15 Bcf of curtailments into our second quarter production guidance." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "From a CapEx standpoint, the second quarter represents our peak capital investment period of the year... We expect to see meaningful declines in capital spending into the third and fourth quarters, which should further support free cash flow generation in the back half of the year." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "It's a little early to update something like full year guidance without a material change otherwise... we'd probably look typically to do that by midyear." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)

Financial results

  • EQT did not disclose EPS or revenue figures in the prepared remarks or Q&A included here, so no comparison to analyst estimates is included.
  • "We generated more than $1.8 billion of free cash flow before the effects of $475 million of working capital inflows." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "We exited the quarter with net debt of just under $5.7 billion." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "The benefits of our opportunistic strategy were on display as we captured nearly 100% of the surge in natural gas prices in the first quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "As prices have moderated into the spring, we are realizing the benefits with our balance of year hedge book in the money by $180 million." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "This accelerated deleveraging has already been recognized by the credit rating agencies, with Fitch upgrading EQT to BBB during the quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)

Q&A

  • Douglas Leggate, Wolfe Research: asked how EQT can improve realizations and whether LNG exposure can be accelerated before post-2030; President, CEO & Director Rice answered, "The only way to solve that is to get exposure to international pricing... we're excited to start trading with LNG in the 2030 time frame," adding, "getting more exposure to that sooner... is not much of an opportunity in the short term."
  • Douglas Leggate, Wolfe Research: asked why buybacks are prioritized versus a larger dividend; Chief Financial Officer Knop replied, "our base dividend has been and will continue to be a key part of our capital allocation strategy... We see the most value upside certainly on an after-tax basis for shareholders being more so in buybacks," while also pointing to bringing back growth when "sustainable structural demand" is visible.
  • Kalei Akamine, BofA: asked about the scale and evolving terms for data center-related gas opportunities; President, CEO & Director Rice said, "we're looking at multiple Bcf a day of supply opportunities," and "they're going to start landing in the second half of this year," while Chief Financial Officer Knop added that already-partnered projects imply "2 to 3 Bcf per day of demand growth" and discussions could take that to "8, 10 Bcf a day potentially."
  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: asked for updates on Homer City/Shippingport and LNG offtake timing; Chief Financial Officer Knop said, "a lot of really great progress on both" but "those aren't our projects, so we're going to hold off giving like specific updates," while President, CEO & Director Rice said LNG agreements are expected to be "more of a focus sort of in that '28, '29 time frame."
  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs: asked whether Winter Storm Fern performance can be replicated and about M&A; President, CEO & Director Rice stated, "This is something that we do believe we are going to replicate," and Chief Financial Officer Knop said on acquisitions, "we see... it's organically" and "our stock is a much better value candidly."
  • Joshua Silverstein, UBS: pressed on curtailment economics and capacity; Chief Financial Officer Knop answered, "We can curtail a lot, a whole lot more than what we are planning," and framed heavier curtailments as more attractive "right ahead of winter because you have so much contango in the curve."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, centered on realizations, timing risk, and capital returns, reflected in questions like "what can you do to improve your realizations?" (Douglas Leggate, Wolfe Research) and requests to make full-year guidance less conservative.
  • Management’s tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and mostly steady in Q&A, using language such as "the transformation of EQT is now complete" (Chief Financial Officer Knop) and "we do believe we are going to replicate" (President, CEO & Director Rice), while declining to provide specifics where EQT is not the project operator ("we're going to hold off giving like specific updates" (Chief Financial Officer Knop)).
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management continued to emphasize volatility capture and infrastructure-led demand pull; the current quarter added sharper balance-sheet milestone language ("within reach by year-end" (President, CEO & Director Rice)) and more explicit curtailment detail ("embedded 10 to 15 Bcf" (Chief Financial Officer Knop)).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized a record free cash flow quarter and rapid deleveraging ("more than $1.8 billion of free cash flow" and "net debt of just under $5.7 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Knop)) versus Q4 2025’s forward-looking setup around Winter Storm Fern monetization and leverage path ("potential for free cash flow in the month of February alone to approach $1 billion" and "we expect to exit the first quarter with less than $6 billion of net debt" (Chief Financial Officer Knop)).
  • Management’s LNG narrative shifted from market-development and buyer interest in Q4 2025 to geopolitically driven urgency in Q1 2026, while keeping timing consistent; Q1 2026 reiterated, "we're excited to start trading with LNG in the 2030 time frame" (President, CEO & Director Rice).
  • Analysts’ focus broadened from breakevens, MVP, and sales strategy (Q4 2025) to capital returns mix (dividend vs buybacks), near-term curtailment strategy, and how fast demand-pull (data centers/power) can translate into volumes (Q1 2026).

Risks and concerns

  • "We began tactically curtailing volumes... to optimize price realizations during shoulder season." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "Like any of these projects, it takes multiple years to get it built. So it can't happen overnight." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "It's a little early to update something like full year guidance without a material change otherwise." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)

Final takeaway

Management presented Q1 2026 as a record free cash flow quarter driven by strong volumes, low costs, and limited hedging, alongside accelerated debt reduction toward a year-end $5 billion net debt target. For Q2, management guided to tactical curtailments of 10–15 Bcf and said the quarter will be the year’s peak CapEx period, with spending expected to decline in the back half. In Q&A, analysts repeatedly pressed on realizations, the pace of LNG access, and capital returns, while management emphasized demand-pull opportunities around data centers and infrastructure, maintaining the post-2030 LNG timing and reiterating that buybacks and selective growth investments remain central to capital allocation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: COVID vaccine report on cutting hospital visits blocked from publication in CDC journal
data.description:
A report on how COVID-19 vaccines reduced the risk of hospital visits has been blocked from publication in the CDC's Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report, despite being vetted through a scientific review process. 

The Washington Post, which broke the story, said stopping a report from publication after passing editorial scrutiny is highly unusual.

"I cannot recall CDC stopping an MMWR report in the publication phase after scientific clearance and editorial review," Michael Iademarco, who was director of the part of the CDC that oversaw the MMWR from 2014 to 2022, told the newspaper. "On rare occasions we shifted the timing slightly to better align communications plans with competing or reinforcing pieces."

HHS spokesman Andrew Nixon told the Post two weeks ago publication of the piece would be delayed, noting it was "routine for CDC leadership to review and flag concerns about MMWR papers, especially relating to their methodology, leading up to planned publication."

He added that Jay Bhattacharya, the current director of the NIH and acting director of the CDC, said there were issues with "the observational method used in the study to calculate vaccine effectiveness." 

However, a study about flu vaccine effectiveness that used the same methodology as the COVID vaccine study was published in March. 

The held study itself found that the vaccinated adults were half as likely to experience ER visits and hospitalizations in winter 2026.

HHS Secretary RFK Jr. is a well-known vaccine skeptic who has advocated for restructuring COVID-19 vaccine policies. Under his watch, the CDC has removed vaccine recommendations for healthy children and pregnant women, fired members of vaccine advisory panels, and paused federal funding for mRNA vaccine and therapy research.
